name: Rahu
description: Rahu is a shadow entity in Hindu mythology, considered one of the Navagrahas (nine celestial bodies) in Vedic astrology. He is depicted as a severed head, with no body. According to legend, Rahu was originally an Asura (demon) named Swarbhanu. During the Samudra Manthan (churning of the ocean), when the gods and demons were distributing the Amrita (nectar of immortality), Swarbhanu disguised himself as a Deva (god) and managed to drink some of the nectar. However, the Sun god Surya and the Moon god Chandra recognized him and alerted Vishnu. Vishnu, enraged, severed Swarbhanu's head with his Sudarshana Chakra before the nectar could pass his throat. As the nectar had already touched his mouth, his head became immortal, while his body died. The head became Rahu, and the body became Ketu. Rahu is responsible for eclipses and is associated with chaos, confusion, and worldly desires. He is believed to have the power to influence one's destiny and can bring about sudden, unexpected events. He is often depicted riding a black lion or a chariot drawn by eight black horses. In astrology, Rahu is considered a malefic planet, but he can also bring material success and power to those who appease him. He is often worshipped to mitigate negative influences and to gain worldly achievements. Rahu's influence is considered powerful and mysterious, representing the darker aspects of human nature and the unpredictable forces of the universe.
subtitle: Shadow planet, Head of the Serpent

keyMyths.title: The Churning of the Ocean
keyMyths.description: Rahu, disguised as a god, drinks Amrita during the Samudra Manthan. Vishnu severs his head, creating Rahu and Ketu.

keyMyths.title: Rahu's Enmity
keyMyths.description: Rahu is eternally vengeful towards Surya and Chandra for exposing him. This causes eclipses when he 'swallows' them.

keyMyths.title: Rahu and Astrology
keyMyths.description: Rahu's position in a birth chart indicates obsessions and karmic debts. He can bring both fortune and misfortune.

worshipPractices.name: Rahu Puja
worshipPractices.description: Specific rituals and prayers are performed to appease Rahu, particularly during Rahu Kaal (inauspicious time) or when Rahu is unfavorably positioned in an individual's astrological chart. These pujas often involve chanting mantras and offering specific items.

worshipPractices.name: Gemstone Therapy
worshipPractices.description: Wearing gemstones associated with Rahu, such as Hessonite, is believed to mitigate Rahu's negative effects. The gemstone is typically worn after purification and energization rituals.

festivals.name: Nag Panchami
festivals.timing: Shukla Paksha Panchami of Shravana month
festivals.description: While not exclusively dedicated to Rahu, Nag Panchami, a festival dedicated to snake worship, indirectly acknowledges Rahu's association with serpents. Special prayers are offered to appease serpent deities.

cultural.modernLegacy: Rahu's influence persists strongly in modern Hindu astrology, where his position in a birth chart is carefully analyzed to predict life events and challenges. He is often associated with ambition, obsession, and unconventional paths, influencing both personal and societal interpretations of fate and destiny. Many still perform rituals to mitigate perceived negative influences.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In Hindu mythology, Rahu's origin is intertwined with the Samudra Manthan, the churning of the cosmic ocean. Initially, he was an Asura named Swarbhanu. When the gods and demons churned the ocean to obtain Amrita (the nectar of immortality), Vishnu, in his Mohini avatar, distributed the nectar only to the gods. Swarbhanu, realizing the deception, disguised himself as a god and managed to drink a few drops of Amrita. However, Surya (the sun god) and Chandra (the moon god) recognized him and alerted Vishnu. Enraged, Vishnu severed Swarbhanu's head with his Sudarshana Chakra. Since Swarbhanu had already consumed the Amrita, his head became immortal and was named Rahu, while his body became Ketu. This event established Rahu's eternal enmity with the Sun and Moon, leading to his role in causing eclipses. Rahu is often depicted as a severed head riding a chariot drawn by black horses, symbolizing his dark and chaotic nature.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The most prominent myth associated with Rahu is his role in causing solar and lunar eclipses. According to the legends, Rahu, driven by his animosity towards the Sun and Moon, periodically swallows them, causing eclipses. This act is considered a symbolic victory of darkness over light. However, since Rahu is only a head, the Sun and Moon eventually emerge from his throat, ending the eclipse. These eclipses are seen as inauspicious times in Hindu tradition, during which specific activities are avoided, and prayers are offered to mitigate the negative effects. Another significant story involves Rahu's influence on individuals' lives based on his position in their birth charts. A malefic Rahu can cause confusion, obsession, and unconventional desires, while a benefic Rahu can bring unexpected success and opportunities. These stories highlight Rahu's complex and multifaceted nature within the Hindu pantheon.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Rahu is not typically worshipped in the same way as other major Hindu deities. Instead, he is propitiated to mitigate his negative effects, especially during periods when his influence is perceived as unfavorable. Rahu Puja is performed to appease him, often involving chanting specific mantras and offering black sesame seeds, blue cloth, and other items associated with him. These rituals are often conducted during Rahu Kaal, a specific period of the day considered inauspicious. Temples dedicated specifically to Rahu are rare, but he is often included as one of the Navagrahas (nine celestial bodies) in temples dedicated to them. Individuals may also wear gemstones, such as Hessonite, believed to counteract Rahu's malefic influence. The worship of Rahu is primarily a means of seeking protection from his negative effects and harnessing his potential for unexpected gains. The focus is more on pacifying rather than adoring, reflecting his nature as a shadow entity.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Rahu's legacy is deeply embedded in Hindu astrology and culture. He continues to be a significant figure in understanding karmic influences and life events. In astrology, Rahu's placement in a birth chart is considered crucial for predicting challenges, opportunities, and unconventional paths. He is often associated with foreign lands, technology, and sudden changes. Rahu's influence extends beyond astrology, shaping cultural practices and beliefs surrounding eclipses and auspicious timings. Eclipses are still regarded with caution, and specific rituals are performed to purify spaces and individuals. Furthermore, Rahu's symbolism as a disruptive and transformative force has inspired artistic and literary interpretations, reflecting his complex and enigmatic nature. His enduring presence in Hindu thought highlights the ongoing relevance of ancient myths in shaping modern perspectives on fate, destiny, and the unseen forces that influence human lives.

shortDescription: Rahu is a shadow planet and deity in Hindu mythology, known for causing eclipses and representing malefic influence. He is depicted as a severed head.
